Now, number five, I want you to remember the power of language. Now, I might sound like a broken record here, but language is everything. It creates meaning, and next to tonality, it's the single most powerful tool you have to control the conversation. Okay, and there are certain words here that are really proven to be far more effective in getting referrals. Number one is I want you to use the word introduce me to versus refer me to. When you say refer me to, it sort of has this sales, money, being exchanged, connotation, versus an introduction is more of a friend to a friend. Number two is I want you to use the word help as opposed to service. Do you know someone who can use my service, or do you have any friends I can help? When you use the word help, again, it takes away the business-like attitude that goes along with giving a referral, and it makes it more like a friend trying to help a friend. Now lastly, number three, I want you to ask them if they have any friends, family, or neighbors. Use those three categories in one sentence, because it tends to almost embarrass someone into action. If they were on the cusp of giving a referral, and they were maybe searching their database, but not that deeply, and you say friends, family, neighbors, well, it's pretty tough to say they don't even know a single one of those people to refer you. Essentially, you're forcing them to jog their own memory by throwing out a very wide net. You're just easing them very slightly down the road. Don't get pushy or so. Come on, friends, family, neighbors. Just say, you know, any friends, family, neighbors that you think I could help with this program I have here, this proctem, or whatever it might be, you get it? So friends, family, neighbors, okay, in one sentence.
